story:
Today’s the big day. I wake up groggy like everyone, but my phone is blowing up: “You know what today is?” “Never thought I’d see the day.” I grin anyway. I made a promise. I’m going to be there. No excuses. I scrub my teeth until they shine and hum while flipping pancakes. The smell drags me back to Sundays with her—she always burned the first batch, claiming it added “texture.” I laugh it off. Not today. Today I keep my word.

I shower hot, sing too loud, and step into the tux I laid out yesterday. Hair perfect. Cologne strong enough to fumigate the house. In the mirror I rehearse what to say, but everything sounds wrong. “We made it here after everything. You deserve all the happiness in the world.” Too mushy. “Remember when we promised we’d always be there for each other? ” Awkward. “You’ve always made a room brighter.” Corny. I decide to trust whatever my heart can manage.

Driving over, the cologne takes the car hostage until I crack a window. Wind mixes with asphalt and memory. Our favorite trip: nowhere in particular, just miles, music too loud, her hair everywhere, our voices ragged. I try again. “Through every season, I’ve carried you in my heart—” Not me. “I promised I’d be there… today I keep that promise.” Flat. “I promise to support you, in joy and in pain—” HONK. The light’s been green. I keep moving.

Inside the hall, people clasp my shoulder, grin, say, “Can’t believe this day finally came.” I laugh, but I’m miles away: our first date, her late entrance, me stunned anyway. We talked until the staff kicked us out. I learned her favorite color, her favorite flowers, the little hobbies that made her her. A florist pins a red boutonnière to my jacket. “They’re gonna love this,” she says. I slip into my spot up front, the perfect view.

Then the music. She appears. Mesmerizing. She wears her mother’s dress, the one she’ll probably pass to her daughter someday. A tear stings; I wipe it before anyone sees. Her brown eyes catch mine, and the whole world drops out. My heart drowns the music.

“Does anyone object?” Silence. “You may now kiss the bride.” The words slam into me. My smile dies. She’s not my wife. I’m not her groom. Her lips meet his. She’s radiant, ecstatic. He has no idea how lucky he is, how lucky he’ll be every morning. Maybe their daughter will wear that dress. Envy burns hot, but I swallow it.

After, I wait until she’s alone. I wear a smile that hurts and hand her a small box. “I told you I’d be here,” I say. “Even if I’m not the groom.” All that practice, and this is what I manage. She hugs me. “Thank you for coming. You always keep your word.” Her smile does what it always does—it untangles me. The ache eases. It was worth it. Every minute of imagining a future I’m not in, just to see her happy.

I hug her once more and let her go—to the dance floor, to her new life, to him. I don’t stay to watch them sway under lights. I turn and leave the way I came. Alone, but not sorry. I kept my promise.
